policy_statement: Maximize the use of unproductive (idle) lands for establishing forest and agriculture plantations.
mechanism_description: Maximizing use of idle lands for forest and agriculture plantations increases biomass and soil carbon storage, helping turn the land sector into a net carbon sink.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement (afforestation/plantations) → ↑ biomass + soil carbon stocks → ↑ CO₂ removals / ↓ net atmospheric CO₂ → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Policies incentivize or set establishment of forest or agricultural plantations on idle/unproductive lands, altering land cover and land management.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased photosynthesis and vegetation growth accumulates carbon in aboveground biomass, roots, litter, and (depending on practices and prior land condition) soils; reduced bare/fallow periods can also lower oxidation of soil organic matter.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Higher net carbon sequestration and larger terrestrial carbon stocks reduce net GHG concentrations (primarily CO₂) relative to the counterfactual, turning the land sector toward a net sink.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased net CO₂ sequestration (carbon removals) through added biomass and soil carbon storage from new forests/plantations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Land cover change to forests/plantations increases carbon uptake and storage, yielding net CO₂ removals.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such actions can also improve resource efficiency (e.g., timber supply) or adaptation (e.g., erosion control, microclimate), the mechanism statement and canonical label explicitly center carbon sequestration enhancement as the main climate effect—an ecosystem-based mitigation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “idle land” includes carbon-rich ecosystems (peatlands, natural grasslands, wetlands) or if plantations displace existing high-carbon land uses, net sequestration may be reduced or negative; if plantations are harvested frequently, long-term storage depends on product pools and regrowth. These do not change the canonical mechanism’s intent (sequestration via ecosystem enhancement) but affect realized outcomes.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that fits “afforestation/plantation establishment → carbon sequestration enhancement” has its principal climate-relevant effect through increased ecosystem carbon stocks and CO₂ removals, which is squarely Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Additional tagging (e.g., mitigation subtype, biodiversity safeguards, adaptation co-benefits, permanence/MRV design) must be determined from policy-specific evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential net-negative outcomes if plantations replace natural high-carbon ecosystems, drain peatlands, induce indirect land-use change, or rely on short-rotation harvest without durable carbon storage; albedo changes in some regions can partially offset radiative benefits; classification remains NBS because the intended dominant pathway is ecosystem carbon sequestration.
key_indicators: “significantly reduce the emission”, “turn it into net sink”, “maximizing the use of unproductive lands (idle lands)”, “establishment of forest and agriculture plantations”
co_benefits: Potential rural livelihoods/jobs, improved soil quality, erosion control, biodiversity habitat, watershed protection.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via regulatory land-use zoning/mandates or incentive programs, but the excerpt does not specify the enforcement/financing mechanism—only the strategic action (“maximizing the use”), so classified as planning-strategy.
